_meta.purpose: Per-city local facts injected into city-service-cost pages to differentiate them from templated content. Improves Google indexation rate.
_meta.fields: neighborhoods (4-6 named), climate, soil, permits, homeAge, contractorMarket, codeNote, landmarks, geographyNote
_meta.coverage: Top 50 US cities by population + a few high-traffic secondary cities

new-york-ny.climate: humid continental with 30+ inches of snow per year, freeze-thaw cycles from December through March, and noreaster wind events that can exceed 60 mph
new-york-ny.soil: bedrock close to the surface in Manhattan but variable glacial till in the outer boroughs; foundation work is rare but expensive when needed
new-york-ny.permits: NYC Department of Buildings (DOB) permits are required for nearly all exterior work and typically take 2-4 weeks; expediters are commonly used to navigate the process
new-york-ny.homeAge: the median residential building was constructed before 1940 and many structures predate modern codes; older brownstones and walk-ups frequently need lead and asbestos abatement
new-york-ny.contractorMarket: labor rates run roughly 40 percent above the national average due to union scale, parking permits, and the cost of staging equipment in dense neighborhoods
new-york-ny.codeNote: Local Law 11 (Facade Inspection Safety Program) requires building owners over six stories to inspect facades every five years
new-york-ny.geographyNote: five boroughs spanning Manhattan island, the Bronx, Queens, Brooklyn, and Staten Island, with very different housing stocks and contractor pools

los-angeles-ca.climate: Mediterranean with mild wet winters and dry summers, but increasing wildfire risk in the foothills and Santa Ana wind events that drive embers miles from active fires
los-angeles-ca.soil: expansive clay soils in the basin plus active fault zones; foundation and grading permits are scrutinized closely
los-angeles-ca.permits: LADBS plan check can take 4-8 weeks for structural work and any home over 5,000 sqft requires a private plan check engineer
los-angeles-ca.homeAge: lots of post-WWII bungalows in the 1945-1965 vintage that often need full electrical and plumbing upgrades during major renovations
los-angeles-ca.contractorMarket: highly competitive but capacity tightens after every wildfire event; verify CSLB license at cslb.ca.gov
los-angeles-ca.codeNote: Title 24 energy code applies to almost all reroofs, requires cool-roof shingles in many zones, and adds 5-10 percent to material costs
los-angeles-ca.geographyNote: spans 469 square miles from coast to mountains; pricing varies meaningfully between coastal Westside and inland San Fernando Valley

chicago-il.climate: harsh humid continental winters with lake-effect snow, freeze-thaw damage to flat roofs and gutters, and severe summer thunderstorms that drive hail claims
chicago-il.soil: heavy clay over glacial till; basements and foundation drainage are constant concerns and most homes have sump pumps
chicago-il.permits: City of Chicago permits go through the Department of Buildings and standard residential roof permits typically run 2-4 weeks
chicago-il.homeAge: vast inventory of brick bungalows and three-flats built between 1900-1940, many with original cedar decking that requires replacement during a reroof
chicago-il.contractorMarket: established trades and strong union presence; off-season pricing (November-February) can save 10-15 percent if scheduling is flexible
chicago-il.codeNote: Chicago Building Code requires Class A fire-rated assemblies on all residential roofing; modified bitumen and EPDM are common on flat roofs
chicago-il.geographyNote: the city's grid layout and tightly-packed lots make staging and dumpster placement a real cost factor in dense neighborhoods like Wicker Park

houston-tx.climate: humid subtropical with hurricane exposure from the Gulf of Mexico (Harvey, Ike, Beryl), oppressive 95+ degree summers, and frequent severe thunderstorms with hail
houston-tx.soil: expansive Beaumont clay that swells and shrinks dramatically with moisture, causing widespread foundation movement and slab cracking
houston-tx.permits: City of Houston permitting moved to an online portal and most residential roof permits issue within 1-3 days
houston-tx.homeAge: huge stock of 1960s-1990s ranch homes; many in flood-prone areas have been raised or rebuilt post-Harvey
houston-tx.contractorMarket: capacity tightens significantly after every named storm; verify TDLR license and require proof of windstorm certification (WPI-8) in coastal zones
houston-tx.codeNote: Texas Department of Insurance windstorm requirements apply within 14 miles of the coast; non-WPI-8 roofs cannot be insured
houston-tx.geographyNote: no zoning code citywide, which creates an unusually mixed housing fabric and makes comparable pricing tricky between adjacent blocks

phoenix-az.climate: extreme desert heat with 100+ degree days from May through September, intense UV exposure that degrades shingles 25-30 percent faster than national average, and monsoon season storms in July-August
phoenix-az.soil: caliche-laden desert soil; rocky and difficult for any excavation work which adds to landscaping and foundation pricing
phoenix-az.permits: City of Phoenix online permits typically issue same-day for residential roofing; HOA approval often takes longer than the city permit
phoenix-az.homeAge: rapid 1990s-2010s growth means lots of 20-30 year old roofs hitting end of life simultaneously, especially in master-planned communities
phoenix-az.contractorMarket: competitive pricing in winter months when snowbirds drive demand; summer is brutal for roofers and many shut down midday
phoenix-az.codeNote: City requires reflective cool-roof coatings on commercial reroofs; residential cool-roof is incentivized but not mandated
phoenix-az.geographyNote: spans 519 square miles across the Valley of the Sun and pricing in Scottsdale and Paradise Valley runs 15-20 percent above central Phoenix

philadelphia-pa.climate: humid continental with cold wet winters, occasional ice storms, and heavy summer thunderstorms that test gutter systems
philadelphia-pa.soil: Wissahickon schist bedrock close to the surface in many neighborhoods; foundation work hits rock quickly which raises excavation pricing
philadelphia-pa.permits: Philadelphia L&I permits typically take 1-2 weeks for residential work and require a licensed contractor on file
philadelphia-pa.homeAge: city has the largest stock of pre-1900 rowhomes in the US; reroofing rowhomes requires careful party-wall flashing detail
philadelphia-pa.contractorMarket: specialized rowhome roofing trade exists separate from suburban roofers; ask any contractor about flat/low-slope rowhome experience
philadelphia-pa.codeNote: Philadelphia requires lead-safe certification for any work disturbing painted surfaces in pre-1978 homes (most of the city)
philadelphia-pa.geographyNote: the rowhouse architecture means most repairs happen on flat or low-slope roofs with modified bitumen or rubber membrane

san-antonio-tx.climate: hot semi-arid with 100+ degree summers, hail risk from spring storm systems, and occasional flash flooding in low-lying areas
san-antonio-tx.soil: thin caliche over limestone bedrock; the Edwards Aquifer recharge zone restricts excavation in northern parts of the city
san-antonio-tx.permits: City of San Antonio permits residential roofing within 3-5 days through the OneStop portal
san-antonio-tx.homeAge: unique mix of 1920s-1940s craftsman in older central neighborhoods and 1990s-2010s tract housing in the north
san-antonio-tx.contractorMarket: hail storms drive opportunistic out-of-state contractors; verify local license at TDLR.texas.gov before signing
san-antonio-tx.codeNote: Bexar County does not require state licensing for roofers but the city requires registration and proof of insurance
san-antonio-tx.geographyNote: Edwards Aquifer recharge zone restrictions affect any work north of Loop 1604 that involves grading or excavation

san-diego-ca.climate: mild Mediterranean with rare extreme weather but salt air corrosion near the coast and wildfire risk in the inland canyons
san-diego-ca.soil: varies from sandy coastal to clay inland; Mission Valley and parts of Mira Mesa have known expansive clay issues
san-diego-ca.permits: San Diego DSD plan check averages 3-6 weeks for any structural work; over-the-counter permits available for simple reroofs
san-diego-ca.homeAge: mostly 1950s-1980s in central neighborhoods and newer 1990s-onwards in the suburbs; coastal homes often need salt-resistant materials
san-diego-ca.contractorMarket: competitive market with many bilingual crews; Spanish-language quotes are common and fully legitimate
san-diego-ca.codeNote: Title 24 cool-roof requirements apply, plus seismic anchoring requirements for any roof structure changes
san-diego-ca.geographyNote: coastal salt air ages metal roofing 30-50 percent faster within 1 mile of the ocean; inland zones have very different material recommendations

dallas-tx.climate: humid subtropical with violent spring hailstorms (DFW is in the heart of Hail Alley), 100+ degree summers, and occasional ice storms in winter
dallas-tx.soil: Houston Black clay that shrinks and swells dramatically; foundation movement is endemic and slab work is a major local industry
dallas-tx.permits: City of Dallas residential permits issue within 1-3 days online; insurance-driven hail repairs can flood the queue after major storms
dallas-tx.homeAge: huge inventory of 1980s-2000s suburban tract homes with composition shingles approaching end of life
dallas-tx.contractorMarket: large hail events bring storm-chasing contractors from out of state; verify local address and TDLR registration
dallas-tx.codeNote: Texas FAIR Plan and many private insurers require Class 4 impact-resistant shingles for windstorm/hail coverage discounts
dallas-tx.geographyNote: the DFW metroplex is the most active hail damage market in the country, with many homes seeing roof claims every 5-7 years

austin-tx.climate: humid subtropical with hot summers, occasional severe drought stress on materials, hail risk in spring, and rare but damaging ice storms (Feb 2021 and 2023)
austin-tx.soil: varies from limestone-shelf in west Austin (very rocky, hard to excavate) to expansive clay in east Austin
austin-tx.permits: City of Austin permits notoriously slow; residential roof permits typically take 2-4 weeks unless filed as a self-issued contractor permit
austin-tx.homeAge: rapid recent growth means lots of 5-15 year old construction in suburbs while central neighborhoods like Hyde Park have 1920s bungalows
austin-tx.contractorMarket: tight labor market driven by population boom; expect 20-30 percent premium over San Antonio for the same job
austin-tx.codeNote: City of Austin Energy Conservation Code requires high-reflectivity roof materials in many cases for residential reroofs
austin-tx.geographyNote: the Balcones Escarpment splits the city geologically; west Austin is on rock and east Austin is on clay, which dramatically affects foundation and excavation pricing

jacksonville-fl.climate: humid subtropical with hurricane exposure from both the Atlantic and Gulf, year-round humidity that drives mold and rot, and intense UV
jacksonville-fl.soil: sandy coastal soils; very few foundation movement issues but termite pressure is constant
jacksonville-fl.permits: Duval County issues residential roof permits within 1-3 days; HVHZ wind requirements apply in coastal zones
jacksonville-fl.homeAge: mix of 1920s-1940s wood-frame in historic Riverside and Avondale and 1980s-onwards tract homes in suburbs
jacksonville-fl.contractorMarket: post-hurricane price spikes are common; out-of-state contractors flock in after named storms and many disappear before warranty work
jacksonville-fl.codeNote: Florida Building Code requires high-velocity hurricane zone (HVHZ) testing for roofing components in much of Duval County
jacksonville-fl.geographyNote: largest city by area in the contiguous US at 875 square miles, which means contractor service-area surcharges can apply to outlying neighborhoods

fort-worth-tx.climate: humid subtropical with severe spring hailstorms (in the heart of Hail Alley), hot summers, and occasional ice storms
fort-worth-tx.soil: Houston Black clay and similar expansive soils; foundation movement issues mirror Dallas
fort-worth-tx.permits: City of Fort Worth permits typically issue within 1-3 days through the Accela portal
fort-worth-tx.homeAge: lots of 1920s-1950s bungalows in central neighborhoods and 1990s-onwards in the suburbs
fort-worth-tx.contractorMarket: shares hail-storm contractor flux with Dallas; verify local address before signing
fort-worth-tx.codeNote: Same Texas FAIR Plan Class 4 impact-resistant shingle discounts as Dallas; verify with insurer before purchasing
fort-worth-tx.geographyNote: shares the DFW hail belt with Dallas; insurance claim cycles drive most of the local roofing market

columbus-oh.climate: humid continental with cold snowy winters, freeze-thaw cycles, and severe summer thunderstorms with occasional hail
columbus-oh.soil: glacial till over shale; basement waterproofing is a major local trade
columbus-oh.permits: City of Columbus permits typically issue within 1-2 weeks for residential roofing
columbus-oh.homeAge: older central neighborhoods have 1900-1940 wood-frame homes that often need full deck replacement during a reroof
columbus-oh.contractorMarket: stable established trades; OSU and state government employment cycles drive predictable construction demand
columbus-oh.codeNote: Ohio Residential Code references the IRC; ice and water shield is required at all eaves
columbus-oh.geographyNote: rapidly growing metro with new suburbs in Dublin, Delaware, and New Albany seeing significant residential construction

charlotte-nc.climate: humid subtropical with hot summers averaging 90 degrees, occasional ice storms in January-February, and remnants of Atlantic hurricanes in late summer
charlotte-nc.soil: red Carolina clay with frequent foundation movement in older neighborhoods, especially in Myers Park and Dilworth
charlotte-nc.permits: Mecklenburg County issues residential permits within 3-5 business days; expedited service available for an additional fee
charlotte-nc.homeAge: many homes built between 1950-1985 in established neighborhoods need full tear-off rather than overlay due to multiple existing layers
charlotte-nc.contractorMarket: rapid population growth has stretched contractor capacity; book 4-6 weeks ahead during peak season (April-October)
charlotte-nc.codeNote: North Carolina requires roofing contractors over $30,000 to be licensed by the NC Licensing Board for General Contractors; verify at nclbgc.org
charlotte-nc.geographyNote: the Charlotte-Concord-Gastonia metro spans into South Carolina, and pricing in Fort Mill and Indian Land is typically slightly lower than central Charlotte

indianapolis-in.climate: humid continental with cold winters, freeze-thaw damage to gutters and flashing, and severe summer storms with hail and straight-line winds
indianapolis-in.soil: glacial till with seasonal high water tables in many areas; basement and crawlspace water management is a constant
indianapolis-in.permits: City of Indianapolis permits typically issue within 1-2 weeks for residential roofing through the Department of Business and Neighborhood Services
indianapolis-in.homeAge: mix of 1900-1930 craftsman in central neighborhoods like Irvington and 1970s-2000s tract housing in suburbs
indianapolis-in.contractorMarket: stable competitive market; insurance-driven hail repair work is common in spring
indianapolis-in.codeNote: Indiana adopts the IRC with state amendments; ice barrier required at all eaves
indianapolis-in.geographyNote: mostly flat terrain makes staging easy and keeps labor costs lower than mountainous or coastal markets

san-francisco-ca.climate: mild Mediterranean with cool foggy summers, wet winters, and salt air corrosion citywide due to proximity to the ocean and bay
san-francisco-ca.soil: varies dramatically; serpentine and bedrock in the hills, fill in the Marina and SOMA (which liquefied in 1989), and sand in the Sunset
san-francisco-ca.permits: SF DBI permits are notoriously slow; residential reroof permits typically take 6-12 weeks unless the contractor uses an expediter
san-francisco-ca.homeAge: huge stock of pre-1940 Victorian and Edwardian rowhouses with original cedar decking, knob-and-tube wiring, and lath-and-plaster walls
san-francisco-ca.contractorMarket: labor rates among the highest in the US; union work and bay area cost of living drive minimum prices well above the national average
san-francisco-ca.codeNote: Title 24 cool-roof requirements apply, plus SF-specific seismic retrofit requirements for soft-story buildings
san-francisco-ca.geographyNote: 49 square miles of dense urban infill; staging, parking permits, and dumpster placement add 5-15 percent to most exterior projects

seattle-wa.climate: marine west coast with constant wet winters (150+ rain days per year), moss and algae growth on north-facing roofs, and rare but damaging windstorms
seattle-wa.soil: glacial till over clay; landslide risk on steep hillside lots and constant drainage management
seattle-wa.permits: Seattle DCI permits typically take 2-6 weeks for residential reroofs depending on whether structural changes are involved
seattle-wa.homeAge: lots of 1920s-1940s craftsman bungalows in central neighborhoods that often need original cedar shake removal
seattle-wa.contractorMarket: tight labor market with strong tech industry wages; off-season (November-February) discounts of 10-15 percent are common
seattle-wa.codeNote: Washington State Energy Code requires specific underlayment ratings; moss treatment is recommended every 2-3 years
seattle-wa.geographyNote: the wet climate means moss treatment, gutter cleaning, and drainage maintenance are ongoing costs that don't apply in drier markets

denver-co.climate: semi-arid with intense UV exposure at altitude, heavy spring hailstorms (Front Range hail alley), and freeze-thaw cycles
denver-co.soil: expansive bentonite clay in many areas; foundation heaving and slab issues are common
denver-co.permits: City of Denver permits typically issue within 1-2 weeks; insurance-driven hail repair claims spike permit volume in late spring
denver-co.homeAge: lots of 1900-1930 brick bungalows in central neighborhoods and 1990s-onwards in suburban Stapleton and Aurora
denver-co.contractorMarket: hail damage drives most of the local roofing market; storm-chasing contractors are common, verify local license
denver-co.codeNote: Colorado does not license roofing contractors at the state level but Denver requires city registration and insurance
denver-co.geographyNote: mile-high elevation accelerates UV degradation of asphalt shingles; expect 25-30 percent shorter lifespan vs sea level installations

washington-dc.climate: humid subtropical with hot humid summers, cold wet winters, occasional ice storms, and remnants of Atlantic hurricanes
washington-dc.soil: varies from clay to gravel; many older neighborhoods built on filled creek beds have drainage issues
washington-dc.permits: DCRA/DOB permits for residential roofing typically take 1-3 weeks; historic district approval (Georgetown, Capitol Hill) adds significant time
washington-dc.homeAge: extensive stock of 1880s-1920s rowhouses with flat or low-slope roofs requiring modified bitumen or EPDM
washington-dc.contractorMarket: limited specialized rowhouse roofers; ask explicitly about flat-roof and parapet experience before hiring
washington-dc.codeNote: Historic Preservation Review Board approval required for any visible exterior work in dozens of historic districts
washington-dc.geographyNote: the District is small (68 sq mi) but historic district restrictions cover roughly 25 percent of the housing stock and meaningfully affect material choices

boston-ma.climate: humid continental with heavy snow loads, ice dam formation a major roofing concern, freeze-thaw damage, and noreaster wind events
boston-ma.soil: bedrock close to surface in many areas, plus harbor fill in Back Bay (which sinks slowly, requiring periodic foundation work)
boston-ma.permits: City of Boston ISD permits typically take 2-4 weeks; landmark district approval needed in Back Bay and Beacon Hill
boston-ma.homeAge: huge stock of pre-1900 brick rowhouses and triple-deckers; ice and water shield required at eaves under MA code
boston-ma.contractorMarket: specialized triple-decker and rowhouse trades; verify Massachusetts Construction Supervisor License (CSL) at mass.gov
boston-ma.codeNote: Massachusetts code requires ice and water shield at all eaves up the slope to a point 24 inches inside the warm wall
boston-ma.geographyNote: ice dams are the dominant winter roofing problem; proper attic ventilation and ice shield placement are non-negotiable

portland-or.climate: marine west coast like Seattle with constant winter rain, moss growth, and rare but damaging ice storms (Feb 2021)
portland-or.soil: clay-heavy with many landslide-prone hillsides in the West Hills
portland-or.permits: City of Portland BDS permits typically take 1-3 weeks for residential reroofs
portland-or.homeAge: extensive stock of 1900-1940 Old Portland craftsman bungalows; many still have original wood-shake roofs that need complete tear-off
portland-or.contractorMarket: tight labor market; off-season pricing in November-February is meaningfully cheaper
portland-or.codeNote: Oregon requires roofing contractors to be licensed by the Construction Contractors Board (CCB)
portland-or.geographyNote: wet climate makes drainage, moss treatment, and gutter capacity critical year-round expenses

las-vegas-nv.climate: extreme desert heat with 110+ degree summers, intense UV that degrades asphalt shingles 30 percent faster than national average, and rare but heavy monsoon rains
las-vegas-nv.soil: caliche over alluvial fan deposits; rocky and difficult for excavation
las-vegas-nv.permits: Clark County and City of Las Vegas permits typically issue same-day for residential roofing
las-vegas-nv.homeAge: rapid 1990s-2010s growth means many 20-30 year old roofs hitting end of life simultaneously in master-planned communities
las-vegas-nv.contractorMarket: competitive winter market when snowbirds drive demand; summer shutdowns are common due to heat
las-vegas-nv.codeNote: Clark County requires registered contractors only; HOA rules in master-planned communities often dictate material and color
las-vegas-nv.geographyNote: the master-planned community model means HOA approval is often the longest step in any exterior project

nashville-tn.climate: humid subtropical with hot summers, severe spring storms with tornado risk (March 2020 outbreak), and occasional ice storms
nashville-tn.soil: limestone bedrock with thin clay topsoil; foundation movement is less common than in Texas markets
nashville-tn.permits: Metro Nashville Codes Department permits typically issue within 3-5 business days
nashville-tn.homeAge: mix of 1920s craftsman in central east Nashville and 2000s-onwards in rapidly growing suburbs like Brentwood and Franklin
nashville-tn.contractorMarket: rapid growth has stretched local capacity; out-of-state contractors flock in after major hail or tornado events
nashville-tn.codeNote: Tennessee does not license roofing contractors at the state level; verify Metro Nashville business license and insurance
nashville-tn.geographyNote: Davidson County and surrounding Williamson and Sumner counties have very different permitting and pricing

detroit-mi.climate: humid continental with heavy snow loads, ice dam formation common, freeze-thaw damage to mortar and flashing
detroit-mi.soil: glacial till over clay; basement and foundation drainage are constant concerns
detroit-mi.permits: City of Detroit BSEED permits typically take 1-3 weeks for residential roofing
detroit-mi.homeAge: vast stock of 1900-1940 Detroit four-square and bungalow homes; many in transitional neighborhoods need full structural assessment before any roof work
detroit-mi.contractorMarket: stable established trades; many homes in disinvested areas have insurance complications that affect financing
detroit-mi.codeNote: Michigan Residential Code requires ice barrier at all eaves; lead-safe certification for pre-1978 homes is enforced
detroit-mi.geographyNote: the city has the largest stock of single-family homes built before 1940 of any major US city, which dominates roofing material decisions

memphis-tn.climate: humid subtropical with hot summers, severe spring thunderstorms with tornado risk, and occasional ice storms
memphis-tn.soil: loess (windblown silt) over clay; bluff areas have drainage and erosion concerns
memphis-tn.permits: City of Memphis Code Enforcement permits typically issue within 3-7 days
memphis-tn.homeAge: mix of 1920s-1940s craftsman in midtown and 1980s-onwards in east Memphis and Germantown
memphis-tn.contractorMarket: affordable labor market relative to coastal cities; verify Tennessee licensure and Shelby County registration
memphis-tn.codeNote: Tennessee does not license roofing contractors at state level; Memphis requires business license and insurance proof
memphis-tn.geographyNote: Memphis sits in the New Madrid Seismic Zone; major earthquakes are rare but possible and affect commercial structural work

louisville-ky.climate: humid subtropical with hot summers, severe spring storms, occasional ice storms, and tornadoes possible
louisville-ky.soil: limestone bedrock with thin clay topsoil; karst (sinkhole) features in some southern Jefferson County areas
louisville-ky.permits: Louisville Metro Codes & Regulations permits typically issue within 3-5 days for residential roofing
louisville-ky.homeAge: huge stock of 1880s-1920s Victorian homes in Old Louisville and the Highlands; many still have original slate or wood-shake roofs
louisville-ky.contractorMarket: stable established market; specialized slate and historic restoration contractors exist for premier neighborhoods
louisville-ky.codeNote: Kentucky does not require state-level roofing license; Louisville Metro requires business license
louisville-ky.geographyNote: Old Louisville has one of the largest concentrations of Victorian mansions in the US; restoration roofing is a real local specialty

baltimore-md.climate: humid subtropical with hot humid summers, moderate winters with occasional heavy snow, and remnants of Atlantic hurricanes
baltimore-md.soil: varies widely; harbor fill in some inner-city areas, bedrock in others
baltimore-md.permits: Baltimore City DHCD permits typically take 2-4 weeks for residential roofing
baltimore-md.homeAge: iconic Baltimore rowhouses (formstone-clad and otherwise) from 1850-1920 with flat or low-slope roofs requiring specialized materials
baltimore-md.contractorMarket: specialized rowhouse roofing trade; verify Maryland Home Improvement Commission (MHIC) license at mhic.maryland.gov
baltimore-md.codeNote: Baltimore requires lead paint inspection for any work disturbing painted surfaces in pre-1978 properties
baltimore-md.geographyNote: Baltimore's rowhouse stock means most local contractors specialize in flat or low-slope roofs and are less experienced with steep-slope suburban work

milwaukee-wi.climate: humid continental with heavy lake-effect snow, severe freeze-thaw damage, ice dam formation common, and noreaster-style wind events
milwaukee-wi.soil: glacial till; basement waterproofing is a major local trade
milwaukee-wi.permits: City of Milwaukee DNS permits typically issue within 1-2 weeks for residential roofing
milwaukee-wi.homeAge: lots of 1900-1940 Milwaukee bungalows and Polish flats; original cedar decking is common and may need replacement
milwaukee-wi.contractorMarket: stable established market; off-season discounts in November-February are meaningful
milwaukee-wi.codeNote: Wisconsin Uniform Dwelling Code requires ice barrier at all eaves and specific underlayment ratings
milwaukee-wi.geographyNote: lake-effect snow can deposit 8-12 inches in a single event near the lakefront, affecting structural snow load calculations

albuquerque-nm.climate: high desert with intense UV exposure at altitude (5,300 ft), wide diurnal temperature swings, monsoon rains in July-August, and rare snow
albuquerque-nm.soil: sandy alluvial with caliche layers; rocky for excavation
albuquerque-nm.permits: City of Albuquerque permits typically issue within 3-5 days for residential roofing
albuquerque-nm.homeAge: mix of pueblo-revival adobe homes (real and stucco-imitation) and 1960s-1990s tract homes
albuquerque-nm.contractorMarket: smaller market with established local trades; verify NM Construction Industries Division license
albuquerque-nm.codeNote: New Mexico requires GB-98 license classification for roofing contractors; verify at cid.rld.state.nm.us
albuquerque-nm.geographyNote: high altitude UV degrades asphalt shingles 25-30 percent faster than sea level installations; flat or low-slope adobe-style roofs are common

tucson-az.climate: Sonoran desert with extreme summer heat, intense UV, monsoon flash flooding July-September, and rare hard freezes in winter
tucson-az.soil: caliche over alluvial fan deposits; very rocky and difficult for excavation
tucson-az.permits: City of Tucson permits typically issue within 3-5 days; Pima County for unincorporated areas has different timelines
tucson-az.homeAge: many 1950s-1970s adobe and stucco homes that often have flat or low-slope roofs requiring foam or modified bitumen
tucson-az.contractorMarket: smaller competitive market; verify ROC license at azroc.gov
tucson-az.codeNote: Pima County and Tucson require registered contractors and have specific pool fence and stucco code requirements
tucson-az.geographyNote: many Tucson homes have flat foam-coated roofs that need recoating every 5-7 years rather than full replacement

fresno-ca.climate: Mediterranean Central Valley with very hot dry summers (105+ degrees), tule fog winters, and increasing wildfire smoke days
fresno-ca.soil: alluvial valley soils, generally stable but with high water tables in some areas
fresno-ca.permits: City of Fresno permits typically issue within 1 week for residential roofing
fresno-ca.homeAge: lots of 1950s-1970s tract homes in older neighborhoods and 1990s-onwards in north Fresno and Clovis
fresno-ca.contractorMarket: competitive market with bilingual crews common; verify CSLB license at cslb.ca.gov
fresno-ca.codeNote: Title 24 cool-roof requirements apply; reflective coating mandates affect material color choices
fresno-ca.geographyNote: hot Central Valley climate makes roof color and reflectivity meaningful for cooling costs; Title 24 enforces this

sacramento-ca.climate: Mediterranean with hot dry summers, mild wet winters, and increasing wildfire smoke days from Sierra fires
sacramento-ca.soil: alluvial valley with some expansive clay; flood plains in parts of Natomas
sacramento-ca.permits: City of Sacramento permits typically issue within 1-2 weeks; County for unincorporated areas can vary
sacramento-ca.homeAge: lots of 1900-1940 craftsman bungalows in East Sacramento and Land Park; 1950s ranch homes in suburbs
sacramento-ca.contractorMarket: competitive market; CSLB license verification required at cslb.ca.gov
sacramento-ca.codeNote: Title 24 cool-roof and solar-ready requirements apply to many residential reroofs
sacramento-ca.geographyNote: wildfire risk in the foothills (Folsom, El Dorado Hills) makes Class A fire-rated assemblies a real concern, not just code compliance

atlanta-ga.climate: humid subtropical with hot summers, severe spring thunderstorms with hail and tornado risk, and occasional ice storms
atlanta-ga.soil: Georgia red clay with frequent foundation movement issues in older neighborhoods
atlanta-ga.permits: City of Atlanta permits typically take 1-3 weeks; Fulton and DeKalb counties have separate processes
atlanta-ga.homeAge: lots of 1920s-1940s craftsman bungalows in Virginia-Highland and Inman Park; 1980s-onwards in north Fulton
atlanta-ga.contractorMarket: rapid growth has stretched contractor capacity; verify Georgia state license at sos.ga.gov
atlanta-ga.codeNote: Georgia requires roofing contractor licensure for jobs over $2,500; verify at sos.ga.gov/index.php/licensing
atlanta-ga.geographyNote: the metro spans 29 counties with very different permitting; Fulton and DeKalb are the densest and have stricter inspections

raleigh-nc.climate: humid subtropical similar to Charlotte with hot summers, occasional ice storms, and remnants of Atlantic hurricanes
raleigh-nc.soil: Carolina clay with foundation movement common in older neighborhoods
raleigh-nc.permits: City of Raleigh permits typically issue within 3-5 days for residential roofing through the development services portal
raleigh-nc.homeAge: mix of 1920s-1950s in inside-the-Beltline neighborhoods and 1990s-onwards in north Raleigh and Cary
raleigh-nc.contractorMarket: rapid Triangle growth has stretched capacity; book 4-6 weeks ahead in peak season
raleigh-nc.codeNote: North Carolina General Contractor licensure required for jobs over $30,000; verify at nclbgc.org
raleigh-nc.geographyNote: the Triangle metro (Raleigh-Durham-Chapel Hill) shares contractor pools but pricing varies meaningfully between cities

miami-fl.climate: tropical with hurricane season from June through November, year-round humidity driving mold and rot, and intense UV
miami-fl.soil: porous limestone with high water table; foundation work is rare but salt water intrusion is a real concern in coastal areas
miami-fl.permits: Miami-Dade County and City of Miami have separate permit processes; HVHZ requirements add review time
miami-fl.homeAge: mediterranean revival from the 1920s in Coral Gables, mid-century modern in many neighborhoods, and modern condos in Brickell
miami-fl.contractorMarket: post-hurricane price spikes are dramatic; out-of-state contractors flock in after named storms
miami-fl.codeNote: Miami-Dade and Broward are HVHZ (High-Velocity Hurricane Zone) — every roofing component requires specific HVHZ approval
miami-fl.geographyNote: HVHZ requirements make Miami-Dade and Broward roofing 25-40 percent more expensive than non-HVHZ Florida counties due to materials and inspection requirements

minneapolis-mn.climate: harsh humid continental with heavy snow loads (50+ inches per year), extreme freeze-thaw cycles, ice dam formation a major concern
minneapolis-mn.soil: glacial till with high water tables; basements and sump pumps are universal
minneapolis-mn.permits: City of Minneapolis CPED permits typically issue within 1-3 weeks for residential roofing
minneapolis-mn.homeAge: lots of 1900-1940 Minneapolis four-square and bungalow homes; many need full ice and water shield upgrades during reroof
minneapolis-mn.contractorMarket: off-season pricing meaningful November-March when many crews are idle
minneapolis-mn.codeNote: Minnesota Residential Code requires ice barrier extending 24 inches inside the warm wall; this is the strictest in the country
minneapolis-mn.geographyNote: ice dams are the dominant winter roofing problem; proper ventilation and ice shield are non-negotiable

oakland-ca.climate: Mediterranean coastal with mild fog-influenced summers and wet winters, plus wildfire risk in the East Bay hills
oakland-ca.soil: varies dramatically; bedrock in the hills, fill in the flats, and the Hayward Fault runs through the city
oakland-ca.permits: City of Oakland permits typically take 2-4 weeks for residential roofing
oakland-ca.homeAge: extensive 1920s-1940s craftsman bungalow stock in Rockridge and Temescal; many homes need seismic retrofitting
oakland-ca.contractorMarket: competitive Bay Area pricing; verify CSLB license; many crews are bilingual
oakland-ca.codeNote: Title 24 plus Alameda County hillside fire-safe requirements; Oakland Hills wildfire requires Class A roof assemblies
oakland-ca.geographyNote: the 1991 Oakland Hills firestorm shaped current building codes; Class A fire-rated roofing is non-negotiable in hill neighborhoods

tampa-fl.climate: humid subtropical with hurricane exposure (Helene 2024, Milton 2024), year-round humidity, intense UV, and frequent summer thunderstorms
tampa-fl.soil: sandy with high water table in many areas; stilted construction common in flood zones
tampa-fl.permits: City of Tampa permits typically issue within 1-3 days; Hillsborough County varies
tampa-fl.homeAge: mix of 1920s bungalows in Seminole Heights and 1990s-onwards in suburbs; many homes elevated post-flooding
tampa-fl.contractorMarket: post-hurricane spikes are common; verify Florida license at myfloridalicense.com
tampa-fl.codeNote: Florida Building Code wind requirements vary by zone; Hillsborough County is in a high-wind zone but not HVHZ
tampa-fl.geographyNote: the 2024 hurricane season hit Tampa hard; insurance availability and pricing have shifted dramatically

orlando-fl.climate: humid subtropical with hurricane exposure, intense UV, year-round humidity, and frequent severe summer thunderstorms
orlando-fl.soil: sandy with karst features in some areas (sinkhole-prone)
orlando-fl.permits: City of Orlando and Orange County both issue residential roof permits within 1-3 days
orlando-fl.homeAge: rapid growth has produced lots of 1990s-2010s tract housing; older Winter Park and College Park have 1920s craftsman
orlando-fl.contractorMarket: post-hurricane spikes are common; verify Florida license
orlando-fl.codeNote: Florida Building Code wind requirements apply; central Florida has lower wind ratings than coastal counties
orlando-fl.geographyNote: sinkhole risk in some areas affects insurance and foundation pricing more than roofing

pittsburgh-pa.climate: humid continental with cold snowy winters, freeze-thaw damage, and severe summer thunderstorms
pittsburgh-pa.soil: shale and sandstone with frequent landslides on steep hillsides; foundation work in hill neighborhoods can be expensive
pittsburgh-pa.permits: City of Pittsburgh PLI permits typically issue within 1-2 weeks for residential roofing
pittsburgh-pa.homeAge: iconic stock of 1880s-1920s rowhouses and four-squares; many in steep hill neighborhoods with difficult access
pittsburgh-pa.contractorMarket: stable established trades; specialized hillside and roof-access skills needed for many neighborhoods
pittsburgh-pa.codeNote: Pennsylvania Uniform Construction Code applies; ice barrier required at all eaves
pittsburgh-pa.geographyNote: steep terrain in many neighborhoods requires special staging and access equipment, raising labor costs vs flat-terrain markets

cincinnati-oh.climate: humid continental with cold winters, freeze-thaw damage, severe summer thunderstorms, and occasional ice storms
cincinnati-oh.soil: limestone bedrock with clay topsoil; landslide risk in some hillside neighborhoods
cincinnati-oh.permits: City of Cincinnati permits typically issue within 1-2 weeks for residential roofing
cincinnati-oh.homeAge: OTR has the largest concentration of Italianate architecture in the US; specialized restoration trades exist
cincinnati-oh.contractorMarket: stable established trades; specialized historic restoration roofers for OTR and the historic neighborhoods
cincinnati-oh.codeNote: Ohio Residential Code applies; ice barrier required at eaves
cincinnati-oh.geographyNote: Over-the-Rhine restoration market drives unique pricing for slate, tile, and other historic-appropriate roofing

kansas-city-mo.climate: humid continental with hot humid summers, cold winters, severe spring thunderstorms with hail and tornado risk
kansas-city-mo.soil: limestone bedrock with clay topsoil; foundation movement less common than Texas markets
kansas-city-mo.permits: Kansas City Missouri permits typically issue within 3-5 days; Kansas City Kansas has separate process
kansas-city-mo.homeAge: lots of 1900-1940 brick four-squares and craftsman; the Plaza area has 1920s Spanish revival
kansas-city-mo.contractorMarket: competitive midwest pricing; hail-driven insurance work is common in spring
kansas-city-mo.codeNote: Missouri does not license roofing contractors at state level; KC requires city business license
kansas-city-mo.geographyNote: the metro spans Missouri and Kansas state lines with different permitting; Johnson County KS has stricter inspections

long-beach-ca.climate: Mediterranean coastal with mild temperatures year-round and salt air corrosion within 1 mile of the coast
long-beach-ca.soil: sandy coastal with some fill areas; minimal foundation movement
long-beach-ca.permits: City of Long Beach permits typically take 2-4 weeks for residential roofing
long-beach-ca.homeAge: lots of 1920s-1940s Spanish revival and craftsman in central neighborhoods; midcentury modern in other areas
long-beach-ca.contractorMarket: competitive LA-area pricing; verify CSLB license
long-beach-ca.codeNote: Title 24 cool-roof requirements apply; Long Beach has additional coastal zone restrictions
long-beach-ca.geographyNote: salt air corrosion within a mile of the ocean shortens metal roof lifespan 30-50 percent vs inland

colorado-springs-co.climate: semi-arid mountain with intense UV at altitude (6,000+ ft), spring hailstorms, severe wildfire risk in foothill neighborhoods
colorado-springs-co.soil: expansive clay in many areas; foundation heaving common
colorado-springs-co.permits: City of Colorado Springs permits typically issue within 1-2 weeks; Pikes Peak Regional Building Department is the main authority
colorado-springs-co.homeAge: lots of 1990s-2010s suburban tract housing in Briargate and northern areas; older 1900-1940 in Old North End
colorado-springs-co.contractorMarket: hail damage drives most of the local roofing market; storm-chasers are common, verify local license
colorado-springs-co.codeNote: Colorado Springs requires Class 4 impact-resistant shingles for many insurance discounts due to hail risk
colorado-springs-co.geographyNote: Black Forest and west-side foothill homes are in WUI zones and require Class A fire-rated assemblies

virginia-beach-va.climate: humid subtropical coastal with hurricane exposure, year-round humidity, salt air, and noreaster wind events
virginia-beach-va.soil: sandy coastal; high water table near the beach
virginia-beach-va.permits: City of Virginia Beach permits typically issue within 1 week for residential roofing
virginia-beach-va.homeAge: mix of 1960s-1980s in established neighborhoods and newer construction in Princess Anne and Pungo
virginia-beach-va.contractorMarket: post-hurricane spikes are possible; verify Virginia DPOR license
virginia-beach-va.codeNote: Virginia Building Code wind requirements apply; coastal zones have higher wind ratings
virginia-beach-va.geographyNote: salt air corrosion at the oceanfront shortens metal and copper lifespan; coastal materials add 10-15 percent to job costs

omaha-ne.climate: humid continental with hot humid summers, severe spring storms with hail and tornado risk, cold snowy winters
omaha-ne.soil: loess and glacial till; minimal foundation issues for most homes
omaha-ne.permits: City of Omaha permits typically issue within 3-5 days for residential roofing
omaha-ne.homeAge: lots of 1920s-1940s in Dundee and Benson; 1980s-onwards in west Omaha
omaha-ne.contractorMarket: stable midwest market; hail repair is a major local trade
omaha-ne.codeNote: Nebraska does not license roofing contractors at state level; Omaha requires city registration
omaha-ne.geographyNote: Omaha is in the central plains hail belt; Class 4 impact-resistant shingles often qualify for insurance discounts

tulsa-ok.climate: humid subtropical with severe spring tornado season, intense hailstorms, and hot summers
tulsa-ok.soil: Oklahoma red clay with some foundation movement; bedrock close to surface in some areas
tulsa-ok.permits: City of Tulsa permits typically issue within 3-5 days for residential roofing
tulsa-ok.homeAge: iconic 1920s-1940s art deco and craftsman in Brookside and Maple Ridge; suburban tract in Jenks and Broken Arrow
tulsa-ok.contractorMarket: tornado and hail repair is a major industry; storm-chasing common, verify local CIB license
tulsa-ok.codeNote: Oklahoma requires Construction Industries Board (CIB) registration for roofers
tulsa-ok.geographyNote: Tornado Alley and Hail Alley overlap here; insurance-driven roof replacement cycles average 7-10 years for many homes

new-orleans-la.climate: humid subtropical with hurricane exposure, year-round humidity that drives mold and rot, intense UV
new-orleans-la.soil: soft alluvial Mississippi River delta soils; foundation settling and pier-and-beam common; below sea level in many areas
new-orleans-la.permits: City of New Orleans Safety and Permits typically takes 2-4 weeks; historic district approvals add time
new-orleans-la.homeAge: huge stock of pre-1900 shotgun and creole cottages in the French Quarter, Marigny, and Bywater; HDLC approval needed for any visible exterior work
new-orleans-la.contractorMarket: post-Katrina rebuilding shaped the modern contractor market; verify Louisiana State Contractors license
new-orleans-la.codeNote: Louisiana requires Class A windstorm rating for roofing in coastal zones; HDLC approval needed in historic districts
new-orleans-la.geographyNote: high water table and historic district restrictions make New Orleans uniquely complex; many projects require both city permits and HDLC approval

wichita-ks.climate: humid continental with severe spring tornado and hail season, hot summers, cold winters
wichita-ks.soil: alluvial valley with some clay; minimal foundation issues
wichita-ks.permits: City of Wichita permits typically issue within 3-5 days for residential roofing
wichita-ks.homeAge: lots of 1920s-1950s bungalows in College Hill and Riverside; 1980s-onwards in east and west Wichita
wichita-ks.contractorMarket: tornado and hail driven; many local crews specialize in insurance work
wichita-ks.codeNote: Kansas does not license roofing contractors at state level; Wichita requires city registration
wichita-ks.geographyNote: central plains hail belt; insurance claim cycles drive most roofing work and storm-chasing is common

cleveland-oh.climate: humid continental with heavy lake-effect snow, severe freeze-thaw damage, ice dam formation common
cleveland-oh.soil: glacial till over shale; basement waterproofing is a major local trade
cleveland-oh.permits: City of Cleveland permits typically take 1-2 weeks for residential roofing
cleveland-oh.homeAge: vast stock of 1900-1940 wood-frame homes; many in transitional neighborhoods need full structural assessment
cleveland-oh.contractorMarket: stable established trades; off-season pricing meaningful November-February
cleveland-oh.codeNote: Ohio Residential Code requires ice barrier at all eaves; lead-safe certification for pre-1978 homes is enforced
landmarks: Rock and Roll Hall of Fame
cleveland-oh.geographyNote: lake-effect snow can deposit 6-12 inches in a single event near Lake Erie, affecting structural snow load calculations
